core_behavior: When a client requests a resource that does not exist on the server, the API endpoint must detect that the requested resource cannot be located.
When a client requests a resource that does not exist on the server, the API endpoint must halt normal response processing for that request.
When a client requests a resource that does not exist on the server, the API endpoint must return an HTTP response with status code 404.
When a client requests a resource that does not exist on the server, the API endpoint must include a structured JSON body containing a machine-readable error code and a human-readable message.
This behavior must be applied uniformly across all endpoints that perform resource lookup.
The 404 response must be returned exclusively when the resource does not exist. It must not be used for authorization failures, validation errors, or other error conditions.
inputs: HTTP Request Method: Any valid HTTP method (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, etc.)
Request URI: The path and optional query string identifying the requested resource
Request Headers: Standard HTTP headers; Accept header may be present but does not alter the 404 behavior
Path Parameters: Variable segments of the URI used to identify the specific resource (e.g., /users/{id})
Query Parameters: Optional filters or identifiers; if a query parameter is the sole means of identifying a resource, absence of a matching record still triggers a 404
outputs: Additional standard headers (e.g., Date, Request-Id) may be included per general API policy.
The response body must be a valid JSON object with the structure: { "error": { "code": "<string>", "message": "<string>" } }

The error.code field must use one of the following defined values: RESOURCE_NOT_FOUND (general-purpose: the resource identified by the URI does not exist) or <ENTITY>_NOT_FOUND (entity-specific variant when the API serves multiple resource types, e.g., USER_NOT_FOUND, ORDER_NOT_FOUND)
The choice between general and entity-specific codes is determined per endpoint at design time and must be documented in the API reference.
Once assigned, a code value must not change (it is a stable contract).
The human-readable message must be written in English.
The human-readable message must not expose internal system details (stack traces, database identifiers, file paths, server names).
The human-readable message must not include the value of the missing identifier in the message string.
The human-readable message must be a complete, grammatically correct sentence ending with a period.
The human-readable message maximum length is 256 characters.
assumptions: Content negotiation is not applied to error responses. Regardless of the Accept header sent by the client, error responses are always returned as application/json. If the API otherwise supports multiple content types, this is an explicit exception for error handling.
Localization is out of scope. All messages are returned in English. Internationalization may be addressed in a future iteration.
Authentication and authorization are handled upstream. If a resource exists but the caller lacks permission to access it, that is handled separately and does not trigger a 404.
edge_cases: If a query parameter is the sole means of identifying a resource, absence of a matching record still triggers a 404.
security_constraints: The human-readable message must not include the value of the missing identifier in the message string (to prevent information leakage about valid vs. invalid identifiers in sensitive contexts).
The human-readable message must not expose internal system details (stack traces, database identifiers, file paths, server names).
